posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
Thinking of opening one in a couple of weeks–it’s the '08, so I don’t want anything too overwhelming. Maybe pheasant?

Thanks in advance!

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked: i’ve been doing 24 hour sv lamb shank and it’s not only easy, but incredibly satisfying and would pair well with the rayas. the texture is much better than a traditional braise. serve with lentils, chick peas, roasted potatoes, whatever.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thinking of opening one in a couple of weeks–it’s the '08, so I don’t want anything too overwhelming. Maybe pheasant?

Thanks in advance!

Try squab pigeon or rack of lamb, served pink. Or even a nice roast chicken or guinea fowl for that vintage.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked: I don’t know what’s overwhelming for a 2008 Rayas, but how about grilled lamb chops?

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked: Provençal Lamb Daube

posts.name: LasseK
posts.cooked:
Well a lot of good suggestions already.
I think duck confit works fine with Reynaud’s wines.

I had the 08 in 2018. I cannot remember the food because I completely dissappeared in the wine.
It is amazing. I challenge anyone to find a better 08 in Rhone.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: Standard pairings with CdP are lamb, duck and game. It works well with beef too, really. I expect your pheasant will be fine, but it is a little more delicate than ideal since by game, they mean venison, boar and the like.

FIFY

posts.cooked:
I would be tempted to just have it on its own to let the wine shine as best as possible. Such a ‘gentle giant’ of a wine - I’d be worried about overwhelming it.

Cheers

I’m not here to compare South vs North, with the difference in varietal make-ups. I’ve not had 2008 Rayas, but I’ve had Jamet’s 2008 CR which I’ve had at release, and have not had since as I am a big fan of aging them longer. In any case, have you had any of Allemand’s 2008?

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Joshua,

In any event, please post your notes - and post ‘the journey’ this wine takes you on from start to finish. I am blown away but how these wines ‘evolve’ after opening, and it’s inspiring as someone who makes wine to experience such singular expressions of a variety I adore so much.

Cheers.

But now i am getting off-topic. I should never have baited… sorry

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
And no way would I pair this with lamb. I like lamb and CDP, but very powerful I think for a Rayas.

That’s a rather extreme generalization! Roasted saddle or rack of lamb are as far from pieds paquets as tenderloin is from oxtail. And all the herb and spice aromas of the Rayas are just a wonderful match with lamb at its most delicate!

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
And no way would I pair this with lamb. I like lamb and CDP, but very powerful I think for a Rayas.

That’s a rather extreme generalization! Roasted saddle or rack of lamb are as far from pieds paquets as tenderloin is from oxtail. And all the herb and spice aromas of the Rayas are just a wonderful match with lamb at its most delicate!

I’ve been scolded! Back to the swamps of Florida for you, country boy!

WTF is a pied paquets!? Sounds illegal. I’ll take one.

Pieds et paquets is a dish mostly thought to be Marseillais but was a famous dish at the Hiély restaurant in Avignon. It used to use cow’s feet, hence the name, but now uses tripe to wrap of a vegetable mix. It is really quite delicious if you are willing to adventure beyond meat and potatoes.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
That’s a rather extreme generalization! Roasted saddle or rack of lamb are as far from pieds paquets as tenderloin is from oxtail. And all the herb and spice aromas of the Rayas are just a wonderful match with lamb at its most delicate!

I’ve been scolded! Back to the swamps of Florida for you, country boy!

WTF is a pied paquets!? Sounds illegal. I’ll take one.

Pieds et paquets is a dish mostly thought to be Marseillais but was a famous dish at the Hiély restaurant in Avignon. It used to use cow’s feet, hence the name, but now uses tripe to wrap of a vegetable mix. It is really quite delicious if you are willing to adventure beyond meat and potatoes.

Historically calves’ feet, but today mostly sheep’s feet! At least in the countryside. When made with sheep, it can be rather pungent, and I remember ordering it, a little over-ambitiously, during a hungover lunch at La Beaugravière where only a 1994 Fonsalette Syrah was capable of standing up to the flavors’ authority.

posts.name: Andrew Taylor
posts.cooked:
Here is a good photo of the pieds paquets The paquets (packages) being sheep tripe…

As to the pairing, a classic with a Chateauneuf is good ol wabbit…go with a lapin aux pruneaux.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Ok that sounds disgusting, but I do like the rabbit suggestion!

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: I love rabbit and, when I’m there, perforce, I have CdP with it, but it’s not the ideal combination. Despite the fact that it’s technically game, I suppose, it’s classified as fowl there and it is more delicately flavored, like chicken rather than duck. Like chicken, of course, it’s tremendously adaptable and dishes that dress it up with mushrooms and other flavors would work.
